items.myth: Generative AI is a completely new field unrelated to classical machine learning.
items.reality: Gen AI is ML with generative objectives and sampling-based inference. Logistic regression and diffusion models share optimization, data pipelines, and evaluation culture—only the target and output form differ.
items.example: A tabular random forest and a small text generator both need train/validation splits and monitoring; the generator additionally needs sampling controls and content safety checks.

items.myth: You must use an LLM for something to count as Generative AI.
items.reality: LLMs are the most visible text Gen AI systems, but VAEs, GANs, diffusion, and smaller autoregressive models are generative too. "Gen AI" describes the capability, not a single architecture.
items.example: A 2018 character-level RNN generating names is generative AI, even though it is tiny compared to modern LLMs.

items.myth: Self-supervised learning and Generative AI are the same thing.
items.reality: Self-supervised learning is a training paradigm (learn from structure in unlabeled data). Generative AI is often the downstream capability (produce new samples). Many self-supervised models are pretrained generatively but deployed discriminatively.
items.example: BERT is trained with self-supervision yet commonly used for classification heads—not for open-ended text generation.
